Programming Languages

Spring 2026 - College Station

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Hyunyoung Lee

Email:  hlee42@tamu.edu

Office Location:  PETR 419

Phone:  979-845-2490

Office Hours

See   Office   Hours   page   on   Canvas   course   homepage

Preferred Contact Method

email

Webpage:  https://people.engr.tamu.edu/hlee42/index.html

Catalog Description

Programming Languages. (3-0). Credit 3. Exploration of the design space of

programming languages via an in-depth study of two programming languages, one

functional and one object-oriented; focuses on idiomatic uses of each language and

on features characteristic for each language. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in

CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent

enrollment.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, students will be able to:

Use modern programming languages effectively

Develop the necessary skills to learn and adopt new programming

languages

Evaluate the power of programming languages and their constructs

Apply language constructs, common abstraction mechanisms, and

efficiency considerations

Analyze the meaning and efficiency of functional and object-oriented

programs

Master many specific language concepts and use them effectively to

build correct and elegant software

Although the course uses and studies specific programming languages,

learning these languages should be considered as a side benefit of the

course, not the main goal.

Special Course Designation

None

Grading Policy

One midterm exam and one comprehensive final exam, each 25%. The midterm

exam will be taken in our classroom during the class time. The final exam will be

taken in our classroom starting at the time set by the registrar. See the course

schedule below for the exam dates and times.

Homework 25%: There will be about eight sets of homework, which mainly consist

of programming, step-by-step explanation and/or analyzing the correctness and

efficiency of your program.

Quizzes 15%: There will be weekly quizzes. One lowest quiz grade will be dropped.

Exercises 10%: There will be weekly exercises. One lowest exercise grade will be

dropped.

Final grades will be assigned according to this scale based on the weighted final

average:

A: 90 and above,

B: [80--90),

C: [70--80),

D: [60--70),

F: below 60

The scale may be adjusted by the instructor to reflect score variations.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Late submission beyond the deadline will not be accepted in general, unless a

university-sanctioned excuse is provided ahead of time. Homework submission is

the only exception: late submission of homework beyond the deadline will incur 5%

deduction per hour late (Canvas rounds up to the hour). For example, if you submit

your work within one hour after the deadline, 5% of your grade will be deducted. If

you submit your work 2 hours and 2 minutes after the deadline, then your grade

will be deducted by 15%.

Course Schedule

Midterm Exam: Thursday, 2/26 in our classroom during our class time (for 60

minutes)

Final Exam: Tuesday, 5/5, starting at 3:30 PM in our classroom (for 90 minutes)

(Tentative schedule; see Canvas Modules for more details. Reading: [H] Haskell

textbook, [J] Java textbook)

Additional Course Information

Course Conduct and Academic Integrity : Each assignment will be done

individually. Note the following acceptable/unacceptable collaborations.

Acceptable collaboration includes: (1) discussing the assigned problems to

understand their meaning or (2) discussing possible approaches to assigned

problems. However, you must explicitly acknowledge any help received from

someone and reference every source you use, whether it be a person, a book, a

paper, a solution set, a web page or whatever.

Unacceptable collaboration includes: (1) copying (verbatim use) of someone else's

solution including AI, physical papers or computer files including program files,

either wholly or in part, however you have access to them, (2) submission of

solutions that are jointly authored, or authored either wholly or in part by other

individual including AI, or (3) providing physical papers or computer files including

program files of your or third-party solutions to other individuals or posting them

on any Internet media.

In general, you may discuss the strategy and approach to solutions with others, but

you must construct and write up all final answers in your own words by

yourself , and you must not share the final product in any way even after the

deadline has passed and even after the course is over.

Make sure that all of your files are properly secured since you may be responsible if

someone copies your files.

Should questions arise during the course of working on a problem, immediately

contact the instructor by email or by office hour visit.

On all assignments and examinations at Texas A&M University, the following Honor

Pledge shall be preprinted and signed by the student: " On my honor, as an Aggie, I

have neither given nor received unauthorized aid on this academic work. "

Submitting any work online via the student's TAMU account assumes that the

contact the instructor by email or by office hour visit.

On all assignments and examinations at Texas A&M University, the following Honor

Pledge shall be preprinted and signed by the student: " On my honor, as an Aggie, I

have neither given nor received unauthorized aid on this academic work. "

Submitting any work online via the student's TAMU account assumes that the

student signed the above statement electronically.

Attendance and Make-up Policy :  Class attendance and course participation is

mandatory.  If students miss class, it is their responsibility to catch up with the

materials and announcements.

Medical reasons or other extenuating conditions beyond the control of the student

must be properly documented. Any make-up work must be completed before the

solutions are discussed or posted or the graded results become available to the

students, whichever occurs first. Discuss unusual circumstances  in advance  with the

instructor when possible. For more information, see  https://student-

rules.tamu.edu/rule07 .

Submission of Work Policy : All assignments must be submitted electronically

using the submission link set up by the instructor on the Canvas course page by the

due date and time specified for each assignment.  E-mail submissions will not be

accepted; they will be ignored without notice .

Regrading Policy : A student can request regrading of any graded material if the

student believes that the points assigned are inconsistent with the quality and

merits of the submitted work. To request regrading the student needs to follow the

guidelines below.

Regrading requests must be submitted to the instructor or using the regrade

request directly on Gradescope if the grading is done on Gradescope, within

one week (unless specified otherwise) after the graded results have been

released to the student. After this time limit no regrading requests will be

honored.

Regrading requests must be in a written form using the regrade request on

Gradescope or via e-mail, accompanied by a reasonable amount of specific

justification and documentation. Make sure that you receive the confirmation

response from the instructor or the TA.

Course Copyright Statement : All materials used in this course are copyrighted.

These materials include but are not limited to the syllabi, exercises, quizzes, exams,

homework problems, solutions provided by the instructor, lecture notes and slides,

handouts, and course videos, etc. Because these materials are copyrighted, you do

not have the right to copy or distribute these materials, unless permission is

explicitly granted by the instructor.

AI Statement

Any use of AI is strictly prohibited in this course.
